title: Allow disabling validation for range input refinements, as it sometimes leads to unintuitive UI behavior
body:
<!--
  ***************************
  Thanks for contributing to this project!

  This form is to report issues or new features.
  As for general questions like "How to hide results when the query is empty?",
  please search or post a question to our discourse forum: https://discourse.algolia.com/.

  In any case,
    - make sure you are using the latest version of the library;
    - do at least one search in current issues or questions, your question might already be answered;
    - do include details, screenshots when it's a visual issue, console errors otherwise;
  ***************************
-->

**Do you want to request a *feature* or report a *bug*?**
Request a feature

<!--
  ***************************
  If the current behavior is a bug, please provide all the steps to reproduce and a minimal
  [codeSandbox](https://codesandbox.io/) example or a repository on GitHub that we can `npm install`
  and `npm start`.

  We have a simple online template for you to use as a base to explain your bug or issue:
  https://codesandbox.io/s/github/algolia/vue-instantsearch-examples/tree/master/examples/e-commerce

  If you are requesting a new feature, we need to understand WHY would you
  need this feature (your use case) or how you are limited with the current API.
  ***************************
-->

**Feature: What is your use case for such a feature?**
AIS automatically calculates the values for the `min` and `max` attributes of the inputs in the `RangeInputs` component. This is convenient, but sometimes causes undesired UI effects. Specifically, when refining results using a facet *after* specifying a range refinement, the browser will apply the `:invaild` pseudo-class if the range entered by the user falls outside the newly calculated value of the `min` and/or `max` attribute.

This strikes me as being undesirable, since it suggests that the user has made an error, when in fact he or she has not.

To see this in action, visit the sandbox at https://codesandbox.io/s/20j9qyzn4n and follow these steps:

1. In the `Price Range` refinement, enter `50` for the `from` value and `100` for the `to` value, then click `Ok`.
2. In the `Color` refinement, select `Bamboo`.

In Firefox, the `to` input for the `Price Range` will now be highlighted in red, indicating an invalid value. If you attempt to submit the form again (without changing the values), the browser will display an error message explaining that the value is invalid. Chrome does not highlight the input, but will display an error message when attempting to resubmit the form.

While this could be avoided by explicitly passing values for the `min` and `max` values, it is useful to retain the default behavior for cases in which users do not follow the interaction pattern that leads to the undesired UI effect.

**Feature: What is your proposed API entry? The new option to add? What is the behavior?**
I would suggest allowing the `RangeInput` component to accept an additional boolean prop (perhaps named `validate`) that defaults to `true`. This prop could then be bound to a `novalidate` attribute on the component's `form` element.

Alternatively, a more granular level of control could be given to the developer by allowing the `RangeInput` component to accept two additional boolean props: `validateFrom` and `validateTo`. These props could then be bound to `formnovalidate` attributes in both the from and to `input` elements. While I can't think of a particular use case for which this level of control would be necessary or desirable, it would be more flexible, but at the cost of a slightly larger and more verbose API.
